title: Cat Eyelid Growths: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Noticing a wart-like growth on your cat's eyelid? Learn about common causes, urgency levels, and why a photo is vital for veterinary triage.
content_html:
What is the growth on my cat's eyelid?

Finding a 'wart-like' bump on your cat's eyelid is a common occurrence, especially in middle-aged to senior cats. Most often, these are benign (non-cancerous) growths such as meibomian gland adenomas, which are small tumors of the oil glands in the eyelid. Other possibilities include viral papillomas, skin tags, or cysts. While these are usually not life-threatening, they can occasionally be malignant, so a professional diagnosis is always necessary.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for a wart-like growth on the eyelid is generally Low. If your cat is eating normally, not scratching at the eye, and the eye itself does not appear red or cloudy, you can typically wait for a scheduled appointment. However, if you notice the growth is causing the cat to squint, if there is yellow or green discharge, or if the eye looks painful, you should move the appointment up to a 'Priority' status.

Why a photo helps triage

In veterinary triage, a clear, high-resolution photo is one of the most powerful tools you can provide. A photo allows a professional to assess the exact location of the mass, its color, and its texture. Most importantly, it helps determine if the growth is rubbing against the cornea (the clear surface of the eye), which could lead to a painful ulcer. Having a visual record also helps the vet monitor if the growth is changing in size over time.

faqs.question: Can I remove the growth at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never attempt to remove or 'pop' a growth on your cat's eyelid. The tissue around the eye is extremely sensitive, and any DIY attempt can cause permanent damage to the eye or lead to a severe infection.

faqs.question: Will the growth go away on its own?
faqs.answer: While viral papillomas in younger cats may eventually resolve, most eyelid adenomas and cysts will persist or grow slowly over time. They typically require veterinary intervention if they begin to irritate the eye surface.

faqs.question: How do vets diagnose these growths?
faqs.answer: A veterinarian will perform a physical exam and may recommend a fine-needle aspirate (FNA) or a biopsy to look at the cells under a microscope to confirm the growth is benign.
